ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb meat food product class; no specific ribloin cutlet raw lean subclass exists. Closest parent class used as the most defensible available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKL covers raw lamb meat broadly; no specific ribloin cutlet lean subcode identified. Closest parent code used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for raw lean lamb ribloin cutlet. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single lean lamb ribloin cutlet with bone weighs approximately 100 g after trimming of all visible separable fat, yielding roughly 70–75 g of edible lean meat.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, ribloin cutlet, raw, lean — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for lamb ribloin cutlet, raw, lean (cr_id F06050019). Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 17174 (Lamb, domestic, rib, separable lean only, raw) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; this substitution is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lamb ribloin cutlet raw lean contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. GI is set to null and GL is 0.0 per standard protocol for zero-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (64 mg/100 g) and high potassium (350 mg/100 g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Lean lamb ribloin cutlet is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium marinades, as its intrinsic sodium is low and its potassium content actively supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means no glycaemic impact; high protein (20.9 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lean lamb ribloin cutlet is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contributes no dietary carbohydrate or sugar, does not raise postprandial blood glucose, and provides high-quality protein to support muscle maintenance and satiety; avoid sugar-containing glazes or marinades.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.6 g/100 g) and cholesterol (67 mg/100 g) are present at moderate levels; lean trimming substantially reduces the saturated fat burden compared with untrimmed cuts.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean lamb ribloin cutlet may be consumed in moderate portions (100–150 g) by individuals with cardiovascular disease, as thorough fat trimming limits saturated fat intake; it should be grilled or baked rather than fried, and paired with vegetables and whole grains to support an overall heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) which can amplify pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production; trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is additionally pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume lean lamb ribloin cutlet only occasionally, prioritising fatty fish and plant-based proteins instead, and when consumed should pair it with anti-inflammatory accompaniments such as turmeric, ginger, and omega-3-rich foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb ribloin cutlet, raw, lean is an uncooked red meat cut taken from the rib and loin section of a lamb carcass, retaining the bone but with all visible and separable external and internal fat removed. The lean muscle meat displays a deep red colour with a fine marbled texture characteristic of young lamb. Per 100 g, it provides 140 kcal, 20.9 g of high-quality complete protein, 6.2 g of total fat (approximately 2.6 g saturated, 2.5 g monounsaturated, 0.5 g polyunsaturated), zero carbohydrate, zero sugar, 64 mg sodium, 350 mg potassium, 9 mg calcium, 2.1 mg iron, and 67 mg cholesterol. The absence of carbohydrate makes it glycaemically inert, suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Its low sodium and high potassium ratio aligns with DASH diet principles for blood pressure management. As a red meat, it contains arachidonic acid and a small amount of trans fat, which are relevant considerations for individuals managing inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. In Singapore and the broader Malay-Muslim culinary context, lamb cutlets are commonly prepared as grilled or pan-seared dishes in mamak and halal restaurants. This record represents the raw lean state before any cooking or seasoning is applied. The cut is halal-certified when sourced from approved abattoirs and is gluten-free and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lean lamb cutlet?
Is lamb ribloin cutlet good for diabetes?
Protein content of raw lean lamb cutlet
Is lean lamb suitable for hypertension?
Lamb loin chop raw lean calories
Can I eat lamb on a keto diet?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06050019, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid disaggregation (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 17174 (Lamb, domestic, rib, separable lean only, raw), which is the closest approved-source equivalent; this adaptation is documented in notes. All other values are exact HPB matches.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — lamb is a pure animal protein and fat source containing no dietary carbohydrate. sugar_g is naturally zero by the same rationale. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no plant fibre. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06050019. 2. FATTY ACID DISAGGREGATION: HPB Foodo does not report saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, or trans_fat_g individually for this entry. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 17174 (Lamb, domestic, rib, separable lean only, raw): saturated_fat_g 2.6, monounsaturated_fat_g 2.5, polyunsaturated_fat_g 0.5, trans_fat_g 0.1. This is an approved secondary source substitution. 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry F06050019. USDA FoodData Central NDB 17174 does report some of these but to maintain source consistency with the primary HPB record and avoid mixing sources without explicit cross-validation, these are retained as -1 pending a dedicated micronutrient enrichment pass. 4. GLYCEMIC INDEX: Set to null (not 0) per protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to not_applicable. Lamb ribloin cutlet raw lean contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. GL is 0.0. 5. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311349 and FoodEx2 code A0CKL are closest_parent anchors; no exact subclass for raw lean lamb ribloin cutlet was identified in either system. Match types set to closest_parent per allowed values. SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note. No codes invented. 6. CUISINE: Input assigned primary_cuisine as mamak; this is retained in cultural context but the food itself is a generic raw ingredient used across multiple cuisines. cuisine field set to Generic / International to reflect the raw ingredient nature. 7. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: This is a generic raw ingredient record, not a Singaporean dish; mandatory multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. local_names fields are null where not applicable.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name 'Lamb Ribloin Cutlet, Raw, Lean'. No vague or promotional language. Multilingual naming not mandatory for a generic raw ingredient record (not a Singaporean dish); G6 satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 140.0, protein_g 20.9, fat_g 6.2, carbohydrate_g 0.0 all present and numeric. Source is HPB Singapore Foodo (exact match, cr_id F06050019). Zero carbohydrate justified as naturally zero for animal muscle meat.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero, confirmed HPB), sodium_mg 64.0 (HPB exact), saturated_fat_g 2.6 (USDA FoodData Central NDB 17174 adaptation, documented), sugar_g 0.0 (naturally zero, confirmed HPB). All four required secondary fields present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) per protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documented with University of Sydney GI Database reference and explanation. Protocol correctly applied per critical rule.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn broader class anchor provided (FOODON_03311349, lamb meat food product) with match_type 'closest_parent' — valid allowed value. FoodEx2 code A0CKL (Lamb meat, raw) provided with match_type 'closest_parent' — valid allowed value. Both documented with gap notes. SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note. No codes invented. Fixes applied per validator feedback from iteration 2.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,450 characters). Covers food identity, preparation state, taste/texture, nutritional profile, clinical relevance (diabetes, hypertension, CVD, RA), cultural context (mamak/halal), and dietary flags.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern, Na:K ratio guidance), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, zero carbohydrate guidance), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, saturated fat and cholesterol guidance). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 6 English variant forms. All strings in aliases_by_language.english appear verbatim in flat aliases array. Self-check passed: every value in aliases_by_language exists in flat aliases. Multilingual aliases not applicable for a generic raw ingredient record.
